data.title: The Downsides Of The AI Revolution
data.description:
The AI revolution and the massive number of financial headlines this paradigm shift has generated in recent years has been a huge driver of the stock market since the beginning of 2023. A lion's share of the gains in the market has come from the Magnificent Seven with AI related names like Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG).

And indeed, the AI revolution has triggered a huge explosion in spending. Big Tech alone is estimated to have spent $240 billion in 2024 towards building new AI data centers and capabilities. This surge of spending has helped power the markets to all-time highs despite a tepid global economy.

Thanks to this AI driven rally, equities are selling at extreme valuation measures using historical valuation metrics (price to sales ratio, Shiller PE, Market cap to GDP ratio, etc..).

Now the AI revolution has the potential to have the same impacts on the economy as the birth of the Internet a quarter of century ago according to many pundits. AI is predicted to provide a huge boost to productivity across the globe. Much has been written about these potential positive impacts. These are nicely summarized from this recent take by Brookings.

Based on current market valuations, this needs to happen for equities to maintain their current levels, let alone rally further. The S&P 500 (SP500) currently is producing nearly record profit margins historically. And these profit margins are facing increasing headwinds from both new tariff policies as well as tepid global growth. AI is going to have to be able to deliver significant productivity improvements to maintain current profit margins in the current environment.

Plenty of ink has been spilt on the benefits of the AI Revolution. This has been a key theme for the market rally since late 2022, and as previously stated a good portion of which has been driven by the Magnificent Seven. This article will focus more on the potential downsides of this paradigm shift and the possible impacts on the economy and markets. However, there are some large potential challenges and downsides to this massive paradigm shift. Here are some that should be on top of mind:

Electricity & Water Demand:

The huge AI data centers that are being built for the AI Revolution require a huge amount of electricity. Goldman Sachs was out with the projection earlier this year that data center demand for electricity will rise by 50% by 2027 and up to 165% by the end of this decade.

How will all this new electricity demand be met? Renewable sources like solar and wind do not offer the reliability data centers need. Coal is dirty and continues to decline as a source for electricity generation in the States and Europe. Nuclear is ideal in many ways. However, new nuclear capacity takes many years and in most cases more than a decade to bring online.

Natural gas generation seems the primary way this new demand for electricity will be met. The U.S. has huge reserves of natural gas and some 2.8 million miles of pipeline across the nation. This should be a long-term tailwind for natural gas producers like APA Corporation (APA) and Devon Energy (DVN) among other concerns. Companies with large natural gas pipelines like Kinder Morgan (KMI), Energy Transfer LP (ET) and Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) should also be long-term beneficiaries.

Then there are the impacts to water demand and the environment. Global AI demand is projected to consume between 1.1 and 1.7 trillion gallons of freshwater annually by 2027 according to Investopedia. This is equivalent to thirty-four billion gallons of water per week. To put in perspective, an Olympic sized swimming pool holds 660,000 gallons of water.

One of the other downsides to the AI revolution is it is crowding out of funding for other types of businesses by venture capital as you can see above. These may turn out to be great investments for the VC industry, but that means less money available to be invested in other parts of the economy like biotech and fintech. This reallocation of capital will result in fewer companies and jobs being created in other parts of the economy. Not to mention, the innovation potential that will be lost or postponed.

Job Market Impacts:

Obviously, the hundreds of billions of dollars going to build out AI data centers will provide a one-time boost to construction jobs as these facilities get built. Anyone with AI skills will be in high demand as well. But what about a good portion of the white-collar workforce?

A whole slew of jab categories has been deemed very vulnerable to being replaced over the coming years. These include jobs like administrative assistants, executive secretaries, office clerks, filing and records management staff, customer service representatives, retail clerks, cashiers and checkout clerks, ticket clerks, inventory managers, fraud and other entry level analysts among many job classifications that could see significant changes over the near-term horizon.

Back in 2023, the World Economic Forum predicted AI will displace nearly 90 million workers globally by 2027. Recently, Goldman Sachs projected 300 million workers worldwide will be discharged due to AI by 2030. A 200-person law or CPA firm could well need 120 people or less to run the same firm by 2027 thanks to AI. This is just one example of how these types of job losses could play out through the economy.

Can new jobs be created fast enough to replace the ones being lost to AI over the coming few years? I have my significant doubts. What impacts will this have on consumer spending and consumer confidence? This is critical given consumers drive nearly 70% of U.S. economic activity. How will the debt-ridden governments of the G7 respond to the huge disruption in the jobs markets?

With the markets at record highs and priced at extreme valuations based on many historical valuation metrics, an investor has to believe among other things that the benefits of AI will outweigh the disruptions this revolution will have on myriad fronts. Color me skeptical for now on that front.

data.title: GBTC: Access To Bitcoin Anywhere
data.description:
A Vehicle for Traditional Brokerages

In today's ETF education piece we are going to be covering the Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:GBTC). For many, Bitcoin and adjacent crypto currencies are not in the immediate range of products an investor can buy if they don't have an account with a crypto currency trading broker. Some of the more popular crypto exchanges would be Coinbase (COIN), Binance or Kraken. Venmo, Cash app and Sofi (SOFI) are other hybrid investment banking/crypto online trading platforms. For those of us that just trade with Schwab, Merrill Lynch or a variety of other traditional investment banks, these crypto trading products are not yet available.

An ETF Wrapper

Believe it or not, being able to buy Bitcoin through an ETF was a long and arduous battle with US financial regulators. For what now seems to be as common place in financial punditry as gold, silver or oil, Bitcoin was until very recently something that only traders on the fringe of fin-tech had access to. Then, in 2024, Gary Gensler and the Biden SEC approved the new ETF securitization of a series of crypto products for mainstream brokerages:

Today, the Commission approved the listing and trading of a number of spot bitcoin exchange-traded product (ETP) shares.

I have often said that the Commission acts within the law and how the courts interpret the law. Beginning under Chair Jay Clayton in 2018 and through March 2023, the Commission disapproved more than 20 exchange rule filings for spot bitcoin ETPs. One of those filings, made by Grayscale, contemplated the conversion of the Grayscale Bitcoin Trust into an ETP.

We are now faced with a new set of filings similar to those we have disapproved in the past. Circumstances, however, have changed. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia held that the Commission failed to adequately explain its reasoning in disapproving the listing and trading of Grayscale’s proposed ETP (the Grayscale Order).[1] The court therefore vacated the Grayscale Order and remanded the matter to the Commission. Based on these circumstances and those discussed more fully in the approval order, I feel the most sustainable path forward is to approve the listing and trading of these spot bitcoin ETP shares.

And now the rest is history.

Grayscale

From private funds and OTC offerings to now being largely a company providing publicly traded crypto products, Grayscale is the largest digital-asset centric fund manager in the world. While other Bitcoin trusts from larger institutions have surpassed the size of The Grayscale Bitcoin Trust, the company itself remains the largest manager that only focuses on cryptocurrencies.

The Strategy

The Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF is a fairly simple product. A passive product 100% invested in Bitcoin that should track the price movements of Bitcoin less fund expenses.

The Grayscale Bitcoin product can boast being the first to market for crypto exchange-traded products. The product was traded as a private (not publicly listed) fund since 2013, originally offered via private placement. This meant that only accredited investors had access to the product under SEC Reg D in the beginning. An investor had to have either an annual income of at least $200,000 ($300,000 if married filing jointly) for the past 2 years prior to the investment, or a net worth over $1 million USD excluding their primary residence. For several years after these restrictions were lifted, this traded as a trust where individuals could buy shares, or fractions of shares, within their brokerage account like any other stock or ETF.

Although the SEC initially was blocking these products due to the perceived level of complexity and risk of the product, the reality is that investors have demand for these alternative stores of value. Widespread demand has tamed volatility. This is no longer a "fringe" product and institutions have even begun to diversify assets into Bitcoin and crypto products.

How It Tracks

Now for the tracking aspect of the fund. As we can see, The Grayscale Bitcoin Trust does not track Bitcoin price exactly, but gets pretty close. Much has to do with the expense ratio and if investors value the fund below it's NAV (net asset value) due to a higher desire to want to custody the asset directly.

Keep Track of the NAV

We can see prior to the fund becoming a mainstream spot Bitcoin ETF, the product traded at a significant discount to the net asset value. Back in the earlier days, the fund traded at a premium consistently. After becoming more of an institutional product for mainstream retail, the product now trades very close to its net asset value and does not need to be watched as closely for premium/discount spreads.

Who Is This Fund For?

This fund is primarily for individuals, like myself. I only bank with traditional institutions and have never opened an account at a crypto exchange or use online banks that also offer these products. While the traditional investment banks are slowly opening their doors to allowing the buying and selling of crypto, as of now, many of the typical investment brokerages do not have a direct crypto buying feature. As more and more advisors begin to recommend crypto and Bitcoin specifically as a portfolio diversifier, this is one of the easiest ways to accomplish that goal.

Those Who Don't Want to Worry About Digital Wallets

For me, this is a huge plus to owning crypto currencies in an ETF. This is the biggest risk to Bitcoin in my opinion, it is a decentralized asset, meaning there is no one in charge and no one to call if something bad happens. If you choose to only hold Bitcoin off exchanges and in your own digital wallet, like those offered by Ledger, Trezor or Electrum, you need to remember a long password called a "seed phrase" in order to access your coins. This is not an issue with exchanges like Coinbase or if you hold the assets in an ETF like The Grayscale Bitcoin Trust. These companies are responsible for holding the keys with sophisticated data backup systems.

The original intention of Bitcoin was to be able to make private peer-to-peer transactions around the world without wire transactions and cross-border settlement. For most of us, we still use fiat currencies and simply would use crypto as a store of value and a portfolio diversifier. Thus these products have helped make the owning of cryptocurrencies safer and simpler, and most of us do not intend to transact with crypto or have a need for a digital wallet. Here are some real life examples of digital wallet tragedies:

Stephan Thomas

Stefan Thomas has just two chances left to get his hands on his $240m (£175m) fortune.

Thomas is a San Francisco-based computer programmer, and a decade ago he was given 7,002 bitcoins as a reward for making a video explaining how the cryptocurrency works.

At the time he was paid, they were worth $2-$6 each. He stashed them away in his “digital wallet” and forgot about them.

Now each bitcoin is worth $34,000, [~118k as of mid-July 2025] and the contents of his wallet are valued at $240m. But Thomas has forgotten the password that will unlock his fortune.

James Howells

In 2013, Welsh computer engineer James Howells mistakenly disposed of a laptop hard drive containing the private key for 8,000[a] Bitcoin in the Docksway landfill in Newport, Wales. Howells subsequently assembled a team of specialists and secured funding to excavate the site, but Newport City Council refused permission, citing the cost and environmental impact of the search. If the coins are discovered, Howells proposes distributing 30% of the proceedings among the council and the population of Newport.

As of February 2025, the missing Bitcoin was worth £597 million (US$751 million).

Those Afraid of Global Currency Issues and a Weakening US Dollar

Finally, this fund is for those that believe that global currency printing and QE policies will one day lead to a collapse of government backed currencies.

As the M1 money stock increases over time, this leads to inflation and a degradation of a currency's purchasing power. Bitcoin on the other hand is designed to be increasingly more difficult to create (through a process called mining) over time where computers need to solve cryptographic equations that consume more and more electricity as the Bitcoin stock nears its end point.

From Investopedia:

-The maximum total supply of Bitcoin is 21 million.

-The number of Bitcoins issued will likely never reach 21 million due to the use of rounding operators in the Bitcoin codebase.

-No additional bitcoins will be generated when the Bitcoin supply reaches its upper limit. Bitcoin miners will likely earn income only from transaction fees.

This in functionality was designed to be counter to how fiat currencies work.

Who Is This Fund Not For?

This is a very volatile asset. For comparison, to showcase the effects of this volatility even more, we'll backtest this fund against The ProShares UltraPro S&P500 ETF (UPRO), a triple leveraged S&P500 ETF that moves 3x to the up and downside of the S&P 500 index.

Now much of this period from January 2016 - June 2025 includes a vast data swath where the fund was only traded over the counter (OTC) and the fund was purely retail controlled. We can see the largest drawdown for the fund was -83.43% from April 1, 2021 to December 31, 2022. While institutions have now stepped in to stabilize, this is also an asset that has no earnings, no fundamentals and nothing to fall back on other than pure demand. The biggest risk to this asset is both not owning it (a 65.89% CAGR over this 10 year period) and being the last person to own it should demand wane for whatever reason or alternatives that come along.

Summary

The Grayscale Bitcoin Trust is a pioneer in getting crypto backed ETFs to traditional exchanges. Now listed on the New York Stock Exchange Arca, this product is accessible by anyone. If one desires pure Bitcoin price exposure to diversify their portfolio in tax advantaged or taxable brokerages, this is one of the primary products for that.

This article answers these three main questions about GBTC:

1. How does GBTC differ from other Crypto investments?

2. What is an advantage of using GBTC for Bitcoin price exposure?

3. Which investors should own GBTC?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Beyond Meat Is Spoiling At An Even Faster Rate
data.description:
Even though the stock market is cracking fresh new highs, the macroeconomic reality of 2025 is far from euphoric. And amid the pinch of weaker consumer spending and vast uncertainty over trade policy, companies that were already struggling prior to 2025 have an even weaker hand to play.

Beyond Meat (NASDAQ:BYND) is one of those companies. It wouldn't be a stretch to say that the plant-based meat company, which during the heyday of the pandemic reached a valuation of $175 per share, is now in an existential crisis. It faces a plethora of nearly unheard of problems: a combination of sharp revenue declines, negative gross margins, and a severely leveraged balance sheet. The stock is down ~10% since the start of January, but it has climbed ~50% from lows notched in May.

I last wrote a sell note on Beyond Meat in March, when the stock was trading at similar levels in the ~$3s. At the time, Beyond Meat had offered a glimmer of hope in returning to slightly positive revenue growth and positive gross margins: but trends decayed even further in Q1, and I fear that the situation will turn further south for the company. I remain at a sell rating here.

That being said, it would be remiss not to discuss the ~50% rally that Beyond Meat shares have staged since May. This is largely due to two related factors: first, Beyond Meat closing an additional $100 million in financing, as well as the company's expectations of hitting adjusted EBITDA breakeven by the end of the year 2026. More details on the financing are below:

I have a mixed opinion on this deal. On the one hand, Beyond Meat certainly and sorely needed fresh capital. Its balance sheet as of the end of Q1 showcased only $102 million of cash, against a Q1 free cash flow burn rate of $30.6 million.

At the same time, however, at current burn rates the new deal really only extends Beyond Meat's lifespan by another year - through 2026, which is supposedly when the company plans to be at adjusted EBITDA breakeven. And yet: when Beyond Meat gets there, will it ever be able to get out from under its substantial debt mound? The reality is that the overwhelming majority of the company's enterprise value sits in its debt profile: at current share prices near $3.50, Beyond Meat has a market cap of $267.7 million. Its Q1 balance sheet, meanwhile, showcases $102.1 million in cash against a staggering $1.14 billion, giving Beyond Meat an enterprise value of $1.31 billion.

The latest financing will be give Beyond Meat total debt of ~$1.2 billion. Even in a very unlikely scenario in which Beyond Meat is able to generate a ~10% adjusted EBITDA margin on its ~$250 million of annual revenue (which is highly unlikely given negative current gross margins), it will take Beyond Meat a very long time to pay off its loans. We note as well that the company only expects to be breakeven on a run rate basis by the end of 2026 - so even if we believe that timeline, it will be awhile until we can expect to see real profitability from the company.

All in all, though Beyond Meat has rebounded sharply from recent lows, the company is far from out of the woods. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q1 recap

Let's now go through Beyond Meat's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Beyond Meat's revenue declined -9% y/y to $68.7 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $75.7 million (flat y/y) by a wide, nine-point margin. Recall that previously, in Q4, the company had briefly returned to positive 4% y/y revenue growth.

This quarter's trends clearly show that the company is in a state of secular decline. The chart below showcases the fact that demand in the U.S. is the primary culprit, with U.S. retail sales (just shy of half of overall revenue) down -15% y/y and foodservice/restaurant sales declining -22% y/y.

The company blamed the sales decline on a chance in U.S. retailers' assortment strategy and inventory ordering. Per CEO Ethan Brown's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

First, as we discussed in our previous earnings call, certain large retail customers in the United States elected to transition plant-based meat from the refrigerated to the frozen aisle within their stores. In more than one retailer, this transition led to an interruption in availability of some of our core products throughout Q1 2025.

As category and macroeconomic headwinds more generally slowed velocities toward the latter half of the quarter, it became harder to overcome the volume implications of these distribution gaps. Looking forward across the balance of the year however, we expect to build back much, though not all, of this and other lost distribution. These gains provide the opportunity, all things being equal, for better retail performance in subsequent quarters."

While the company is trying to pass these headwinds off as temporary in nature, I'm not convinced that Beyond Meat has the capability to return back to positive growth from double-digit declines in the U.S. This is especially true in a tough macroeconomy, where Beyond Meat products are typically priced at a premium to many standard meat offerings.

The company's problems also extend to the gross margin line, unfortunately. Beyond Meat's gross margin slid to -1.5% in Q1, a six-point slide versus a 4.9% margin in the year-ago quarter.

Again, the company tried to pass off some of the weakness as one-time. As shown in the table below, Beyond Meat is calling out $5.2 million of gross margin unfavorability due to excess and obsolete inventory, worth about 7.6 points of margin (without which gross margins would have been positive).

There are two problems with this approach, however. Excess/obsolete inventory as a result of declining demand is certainly a "real" headwind to gross margins. Second, recall that in Beyond Meat's original FY25 outlook, the company had originally called for "approximately 20%" gross margins. Even after stripping out these supposed one-time items, Beyond Meat would still fall short of its original targets.

Unsurprisingly, Beyond Meat has withdrawn its guidance for FY25. And yet, the company has established a track record for providing unreliable estimates. Are we truly to believe that Beyond Meat can really hit breakeven adjusted EBITDA as soon as next year, when gross margins are still negative?

Adjusted EBITDA in Q1 clocked in at a loss of -$42.3 million, which is a -62% margin.

In short: I think Beyond Meat has a steep hill to climb to get to where it needs to be.

Q2 expectations and key takeaways

Beyond Meat is expected to report Q2 results in early August. The key items for us to watch for are growth/decline trends in the U.S. retail and foodservice channels (was the switch from the refrigerated aisle to the frozen section truly a one-time drag, or are there deeper demand issues at play?) We should also be on the lookout for any potential recovery in gross margins, as the company insists that its investments in a new distribution facility in Pennsylvania will yield cost improvements.

In my view, we're more likely to see a continuation of weak growth trends and margin decay. The company's $100 million in fresh funding is just a temporary stopgap that balloons its debt load.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: PAVE: Continued Industrials And Materials Tailwinds, Eye On The Macro
data.description:
Industrials is one of two S&P 500 sector ETFs to notch record highs through last Thursday. Information Technology is hot on its heels, as investors flock back to the mega-caps. Still, other areas have performed well and are attracting investors’ attention...and cash. BofA noted that there have been record inflows to the cyclical Materials sector.

Industrials and Materials comprise the bulk of the Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF (BATS:PAVE) portfolio. I had a buy rating on the product back in February. Shares are up 8%, dividends included, since then, and I reiterate a buy rating.

Industrials Best S&P 500 Sector ETF YTD

Record Inflow to Materials

According to the issuer, PAVE seeks to invest in companies that stand to benefit from a potential increase in infrastructure activity in the United States, including those involved in the production of raw materials, heavy equipment, engineering, and construction. The fund invests in growth and value stocks of companies across diversified market capitalization.

PAVE is a large ETF with $9.0 billion in assets under management as of July 17, 2025. That’s about unchanged from February, despite the notable advance. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 47 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at just 0.56%.

Share-price momentum has turned much improved, earning PAVE a strong A ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system; that’s up from a tepid C rating just three months ago. But PAVE can be risky, as evidenced by elevated realized volatility metrics and its economically exposed allocation. Finally, liquidity is good—average daily volume is north of two million shares, while its 30-day median bid/ask spread is tight at only two basis points, per Global X ETFs.

For an update on the portfolio, the 5-star ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. One-third of the allocation is found in mid-cap blend, and there’s just 22% access to large caps. There is a slight bent toward growth, but prospective investors should expect this fund to move alongside expectations about the broader economy. As it stands, there’s just a 1-in-5 implied chance of a US recession this year, per prediction markets, and interest rates remain below key resistance levels.

On valuation, PAVE has turned a bit pricier, as measured by the price-to-earnings ratio, which is now near 22x. At the time of my previous assessment, the P/E was near 20x. Still, the ETF has a slightly below-market multiple.

PAVE: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Small Chance of a US Recession in 2025

Industrials and Materials command a high 93% of PAVE’s sector mix. You’ll find some major YTD winners at the top of the holding stack, including Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and Fastenal (FAST). The earnings season will be crucial to how the ETF performs, and the next three weeks are peak reporting times for Industrials and Materials.

PAVE: Holdings & Dividend Information

Industrials & Materials Earnings On Tap

Seasonally, PAVE tends to do very well in July, but weaker price action has occurred in August and September.

PAVE: Weaker Aug-Sept Trends

The Technical Take

With the fund still at a modest P/E discount to the S&P 500 and with key earnings reports on tap, the technical situation is strong. Notice in the chart below that the fund remains below its all-time high notched in 2024, but it’s approaching that spot. Following a bullish golden cross pattern, in which the long-term 200-day moving average rises above the shorter-term 50dma, the bulls control the primary trend. Moreover, there’s now a high amount of volume by price under the current level.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—it has been ranging in a bullish zone between 60 and 75. A rally through $46 could portend an upside measured move price objective to about $64 based on the depth of the December to April decline.

PAVE: All-Time High In Sight, Support Near $40

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on PAVE. While much hinges on the strength of the economy, this cyclical fund sports strong momentum as money comes into the Industrials and Materials sectors.

data.title: A Second Inflation Wave May Be Coming And Could Shock The Markets
data.description:
Inflation expectations are now starting to become unmoored. It seems hard to argue that when a 5-year CPI swap breaks out of a 2-year base, it isn't a problem. It will have significant consequences as well, because it will ultimately mean that interest rates are likely to rise rather significantly.

Given how tight credit spreads are and how high stock market valuations are, the market is not prepared for what the CPI swap market is predicting.

CPI Swaps Breakout

It's hard to imagine that a breakout in inflation expectations won't have an impact on interest rates, either, because 10- (US10Y) and 30-(US30Y) year Treasuries have traded in lockstep with CPI swaps for a long time, so the effects are likely to be felt across the market.

The cause is likely related to tariffs, rising commodity prices, and possibly increasing shipping rates. The Baltic Dry Index surged above 2,000 for the first time since the fall of 2020. With rising commodity prices, such as copper (HG1:COM) and, more importantly, iron ore (SCO:COM) (which appears poised to break out), the Baltic Dry Index is likely to continue increasing. Historically, copper and iron ore prices tend to lead the Baltic Dry Index.

All of this is likely a contributing factor, but what is undeniable is that the 5-yr CPI Swap is now trading at its highest level since the spring of 2023. It shouldn't be surprising, either, since CPI swaps tend to focus on copper over the long term. So with copper breaking out to a new all-time high following the announcement of a 50% tariff, CPI swaps are following along.

If CPI swaps continue to climb, it will likely only add to the recent pressure being seen on the back of the yield curve. Treasury rates, such as the 10-year, tend to trade in line with CPI swap pricing. The recent surge in CPI swap pricing suggests that the 10-year rate should be heading higher, and potentially considerably higher, perhaps not only to their highs in October 2023 but possibly beyond.

It is a problem, because 1-year CPI swaps are trading at higher levels than the 5-year swaps, and one needs to go back to July 2022 to find a higher pricing. Meanwhile, 2-year CPI swaps are also breaking out of that same trading range.

The Market's Warning

While one can argue about the best way to measure inflation expectations, it seems that swap pricing could be one of the better options, as a transaction is taking place. When examining the inflation market, it suggests that after two years of consolidation, inflation has not only bottomed but is also heading higher.

This isn't really a matter of opinion; it's what the market is screaming. The market can be wrong, and it has the right to change its mind; however, the risk here is that inflation resurfaces, which means that long-end rates will have to climb much further.

The market, overall, from rates to stocks and credit, is not prepared for a second inflation spike. Valuations are too, and spreads are too tight, the entire market is priced for perfection, and we all know we live in a far-from-perfect world.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Economic Conditions Are Not That Bad
data.description:
The Friday edition of the Wall Street Journal starts out this way:

"A strong start to earnings season is helping offset investors' worries over tariffs and threats to the Federal Reserve’s independence."

"Stock futures edged upward after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite both notched record highs Thursday. (There have been six new historical highs reached by these measures over the past two weeks.) Financial updates from banks and consumer-focused companies this week have pointed to a resilient U.S. economy. Netflix lifted its annual forecasts late Thursday as it continues to see membership growth."

And, President Trump wants to fire Fed Chairman Jerome Powell?

Since the middle of April this year, the S&P 500 Stock Index has posted the following trail.

Yes, there is plenty of uncertainty floating around. Yes, people are not happy with all the tariff negotiations. Yes, people don't like many of the personal appointments and personal decisions coming out of the White House.

But....

The general picture of the U.S. economy is not bad. In fact, some dare to say that the general picture of the U.S. economy is alright.

Go figure.

And, the Federal Reserve continues its program of quantitative tightening.

Here is what the Fed has done to its portfolio of securities held outright since the most recent change...on September 18, 2024...in the severity of the reduction program.

The reduction in the portfolio of securities held outright seems to follow right along with the Fed's intention to keep shrinking the size of this portfolio.

Let me just say that the reductions taking place right now are nowhere near the magnitude of the reduction taken in the earlier part of the quantitative tightening effort.

But the Fed has continued this process, and we see it now in its 36th month.

Wow!

I don't remember ANY Federal Reserve policy stance lasting for such a long period of time.

And, the results?

The results have been phenomenal. Rising stock prices. Adequate economic growth. Falling inflation. Unemployment remaining relatively steady. Dollar value is maintained in foreign exchange markets.

In fact, looking at the economy and looking at the role the Federal Reserve has played in the results cited, more and more people are arguing that, as far as the Federal Reserve is concerned, as far as the economy is concerned, and as far as the overall picture for what is going on in the economy, the president should play his TACO card. TACO equals Trump Always Chickens Out!

In other words, Trump needs to back off his criticism of Chairman Powell and, even if he does not give Mr. Powell credit for what has been accomplished, move to a more accepting position regarding where the economy is.

I know that this might be difficult for the president to do, but in this case, he would be acting in a way that would be the most helpful to him and his efforts to Make America Great Again.

Trump would still like the Federal Reserve to lower its policy rate of interest. Although at times, Federal Reserve officials have talked about lowering the Fed's policy rate of interest, no one really seems to be taking an active role in seeking a reduction in the rate.

Here is what the rate situation looks like since September 18, 20024.

The failure to reduce the policy rate of interest is not because the banking system does not have ample reserve balances on hand.

In fact, the current commercial banking system has a massive amount of "excess" reserves on hand that they could loan out and generate some real inflationary pressure.

Take a look.

The commercial banks have roughly $3.375 trillion in "excess reserves" on their balance sheets.

This is a massive amount.

The argument could be made that the Federal Reserve could lower its policy rate of interest. There certainly is sufficient "liquidity" around in the commercial banking system to support a lower rate of interest.

But, Federal Reserve officials continue to argue that in terms of where they want to see the banking system go, it is not quite time for the policy rate to be dropped.

We are close, however. The policymakers could lower the policy rate of interest, but at present, they are just not quite ready to open the door and see interest rates decline.

To me, it seems that the Fed is concerned about what a drop in the Fed's policy rate of interest would generate in the open market.

Federal Reserve officials remain very concerned about moving too fast and opening the door to greater rates of inflation if they do.

The Federal Reserve has worked this hard to get inflation down to where it is. I believe that the concern is that Fed officials are afraid that moving too soon to lower rates will reignite inflation and all the hard work that has been done...will have to be repeated.

The call within the Federal Reserve is to err on the side of too much caution.

The rate of inflation has been falling. The rate of inflation is relatively low. Don't move too fast. Don't make people go through this painful process again.

Yet, the politicians want the suffering to end. They want to begin stimulating again in order to turn people around, make them happier with lower interest rates, and with more economic activity. And, the politicians want to get re-elected.

The battle will go on.

Whatever, economic conditions are not too bad. The stock market keeps hitting new historical highs.

And, the Federal Reserve continues its efforts to remove all the "excess" monies it injected into the banking system during the CovidCOVID-19emic and the subsequent period when the economy went into recession.

And, President Trump pushes for reducing interest rates.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Crypto Clarity, Financial Earnings, JNJ And JOBY
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Crypto legislation back on track, Bitcoin hitting highs (0:20). Financial earnings holding ground (2:00). JNJ beats expectations, absorbing tariff hits (3:25). Tesla report coming next week; news priced in (4:15). Consumer earnings coming, (5:50). Joby Aviation back on investors' radar (9:15).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome back to another week of Wall Street Roundup. Thanks for joining us, Brian.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here as usual.

RS: So lead us into the weekend. Talk to us about what we've seen this week.What's been top of mind for you?

BS: This week, there's been a lot of wrangling in Washington. There were some hurdles with the crypto regulation legislation that's been making its way through Congress, but that seems back on track now.

You see Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has been higher lately though. It's had trouble getting above the $120,000 mark. So I would keep that in mind as a spot.

But if you look at the shovels and pick axe level of Bitcoin related stocks, you see Coinbase (COIN) is up 5% in the last five days. It's setting a new fifty two week high. It's up 67% year to date.

Meanwhile, Circle (CRCL) is up 23% over the past five days. So this is during the period of time when the politicians were ironing out the legislation, Circle's off its post IPO highs, which it reached in June. But like I said, still up strongly during the week.

So I think you see some upside in the overall crypto space just as there gets to be some clarity about the regulatory environment.

After Trump was elected, there was a spike in Bitcoin and crypto stocks, with the belief that there would be a supportive regulatory environment, and you're starting to see that actually come to pass. I think you're just fulfilling some of the promise that was made a few months ago going into this administration.

RS: And earning season is well underway. Yesterday, you were talking about the airlines. What would you say from the financial sector?

BS: Financials were the main theme of earnings this week. We're still ramping up in terms of earning season, so there's not a lot of huge names outside of financials.

It was a relatively unimpressed market. It was basically a shrug following the financial earnings. So you see most of the major players up or down in a range of plus or minus 2% following their results.

One standout is Citi (C), which is up about 5% in the the last five days, but overall, a flat performance. If you look at the (XLF), which is the sector ETF for financials, it's basically flat over the last five days.

So the market took those earnings in stride. They're in line with what people expected. And you have to remember that financials were up going into it. So names like Citi were up 29% year to date. Goldman Sachs (GS) was up 24%. The overall XLF was up 8% year to date.

I think that as a kickoff to the earning season, I would take that as a win that the already strong performing financial sector held its ground after its earnings. So that part of it seems like it's well within expectations.

Otherwise, you saw J&J (JNJ) rose about 6% after its earnings. At one point, it hit the biggest intraday gain in nearly two years. Following that, it beat expectations, on both earnings and revenue, raised its forecasts. It cited forks tailwinds and strong operational performances for its earnings.

So far, it's been able to absorb the tariff hit. Obviously, that's something to keep an eye on for big multinational companies like JNJ, whether or not the tariffs that are currently coming online are going to have an impact.

So far so good on that front, at least using JNJ as a a bellwether for that, but I think we'll see as the earning season heats up how concerned companies are about that.

RS: Anything else to add to the earnings conversation or what investors could look for next week?

BS: So next week, the big name is going to be Tesla (TSLA). As always, it's going to be a tension between results and the future prospects.

Investors are already aware that the company has taken a hit recently, both the brand hit that it took with Elon Musk, DOGE, and then the feud with Trump, the whole drama there obviously put Tesla in the spotlight in a way that wasn't exactly beneficial to its brands.

That news is largely priced into the stock. At this point I think investors, like a bullish investor, would be betting that it bottomed out in Q1 and Q2 is gonna show signs of recovery there. So I think avoiding a disaster on that front would be step one for a Tesla bull.

And then I think a lot of the discussion around the stock is going to focus on its more innovative products. Humanoid robots, robotaxis, things like that. Look out for commentary for that. In a lot of ways, that's where Musk excels as a businessman is the showmanship aspect of it.

The earnings conference call and the presentation there is probably gonna give him a chance to reset expectations and kind of get back into consciousness, at least on Wall Street, as a businessman and less as a political player.

RS: Anything else to add to the conversation to look forward to for next week, either macro or other stock specific news happening?

BS: Yeah. Just to look at a few themes that'll likely come up. There's some consumer players that are set to report earnings. Coca Cola (KO) is coming out, Southwest (LUV), is coming out in the airline space.

You have AutoNation (AN), an auto dealer. That might be an interesting look at whether or not consumers are putting off purchases of large items due to the current pricing environments.

Meanwhile, on Friday, you have (HCA), which is a hospital operator coming out with the commentary coming out of the big beautiful bill about the situation for hospitals, rural hospitals specifically, but hospitals in general. I think that earnings call will be interesting in that industry just to see where industry leaders are looking for the future in the new regulatory and federal spending environment.

RS: Coke (KO) was in the news this week, hit some headlines with the president. Anything to note there?

BS: The environment we're in, there's such an overlap between Washington and Wall Street. I think that's gonna be inevitable from here on out is there's gonna be feuds that spark up.

Disney (DIS) was the kind of political target for a while. I don't think we've seen the last of of that either. I think investors are getting used to that as part of the noise surrounding stock trading now is just where do certain big name brands stand in regard to the administration.

RS: And there's always been, right, that sort of tension at times between Wall Street and Washington. Do you feel like it's the most pronounced now or do you feel it's just the most nakedly displayed, let's say?

BS: I think the tone is different because you can think back to during the Obama administration, during the Bailout era, there was a huge overlap between Washington and Wall Street. It was more directly financial related. It was also in the wake of a major crisis, so it was predictable that the government will get involved.

Also thinking back to the Clinton administration, there was a lot of talk about antitrust in Microsoft (MSFT) and breaking up the company, perhaps things like that.

It's not certainly unheard of that there would be tension or disputes that rise up among large companies and the federal government, but the tone does seem to have changed. It's more personal now than it was. It's less regulatory and more political in nature.

So again, I feel like since the first round of tariff stock movements, in the April time frame when the market crashed on the fear that the tariffs were gonna come to effect and then the bounce back that happened when those were delayed, even now when those headlines are coming out, you don't see the huge swings now that you saw back then.

I feel like investors are becoming immune to it in a certain way. They're sort of building it into their mental models on how to invest, that this is just the tone at which these discussions are going to continue for at least the next few years.

RS: Bob and adapt. Anything else, Brian?

BS: One company that I just thought was worth noting because it's kinda interesting. Joby (JOBY). It makes electronic air taxis. It's up about 39% in the past week. It is set to double its production in its California facility. It's going to be able to make up to 24 air taxis.

This is a company that came public a few years ago in 2021 through a SPAC. It got a lot of initial excitement and then fell off dramatically when it became clear that it wasn't gonna meet ambitious timelines that it set forth.

Their goal is to have an air taxi service. The flagship that they've been talking about for a while is from lower Manhattan to JFK where they can fly you there in I think seven minutes or something like that was the time frame given.

That hasn't materialized yet, but the recent moves that the company's made upgrading its facilities, there's also been some regulatory easing. They're looking to launch their first service in Dubai. So there's signs that the company is materializing some of the promise they had and so it's been up dramatically lately.

So just as a check-in from a company that was hot a while ago and kind of fell off the radar, I think it's interesting to note that it's back on the radar.
